Summary: In cohort studies (Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study, 2007; French Epidemiological Study on the Insulin Resistance Syndrome, 2011), TCF7L2 risk variants predicted T2D onset and were associated with impaired glucose regulation. In the French cohort, TCF7L2 variants associated with faster reduction of pancreatic β‑cell function, decreased insulin secretion and increased glycemia. Physiological studies found variant rs290487 contributed to insulin resistance and implicated Wnt signaling in that resistance. These effects related to familial history of T2D and indicate familial genetic predisposition to developing impaired glycemia and T2D over time. TCF7L2 variants are consistently associated with T2D across multiple populations with diverse genetic origins. A meta-analysis indicated these variants act under a multiplicative genetic model and suggested TCF7L2 is involved in nearly one‑fifth of T2D cases. The relevance of ethnic differences remains to be determined. Resequencing in diverse ethnic groups can identify common and rare variants and functional and physiological follow‑up can reveal diabetes‑related molecular pathways. The role of TCF7L2 variants in complex regulatory functions is little explored and requires further study. Developing therapies that modulate TCF7L2 expression may provide opportunities to treat T2D patients carrying risk alleles.
References: Del Bosque-Plata, L., Martínez-Martínez, E., Espinoza-Camacho, M. Á., & Gragnoli, C. (2021). The Role of TCF7L2 in Type 2 Diabetes. Diabetes, 70(6), 1220-1228. https://doi.org/10.2337/db20-0573
